content.text:
on an existing Ubuntu VM, I ran chezmoi init again and set the stack to full and personal to true. This is the problem I got.

Already up to date.
Enter the password for hussainweb@gmail.com at my.1password.com: 
Enter the password for hussain@axelerant.com at axelerant.1password.com: 
Updating and installing APT packages...
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
Reading state information... Done
curl is already the newest version (8.18.0-1ubuntu2.1).
git is already the newest version (1:2.53.0-1ubuntu1).
fish is already the newest version (4.2.1-3.2).
zsh is already the newest version (5.9-8ubuntu3).
Solving dependencies... Done
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 0 to remove and 6 not upgraded.
Installing NPM global packages...
sudo: 'npm': command not found
chezmoi: install_packages.sh: exit status 1

thoughts.subject: Investigating installation failures
thoughts.description: It appears `chezmoi init` encountered an issue on an Ubuntu VM due to a missing `npm` command during package installation. I'm looking into the specific dependencies and execution flow to pinpoint the root cause of this `npm` command not found error.

The error occurred in `install_packages.sh`, which is likely generated from `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl`. I will start by examining the template script and the package configuration.

## 📂 Topic: **Researching npm command error**

**Summary:**
I am investigating why the `npm` command is missing during the `chezmoi init` process on an Ubuntu VM. The error occurred in `install_packages.sh`, which is likely generated from `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl`. I will start by examining the template script and the package configuration.

> [!STRATEGY]
> **Intent:** Investigating the 'npm' command not found error in chezmoi installation script.
toolCalls.description: Update topic to: "Researching npm command error"

thoughts.description: I'm currently analyzing a Linux-specific block within the script, specifically focusing on how it conditionally installs NPM global packages. My aim is to understand the execution flow based on the `.stack` variable and the iteration over `.packages.linux.npm`.

packages:
  # Homebrew Taps
  taps:
    - ddev/ddev
    - entireio/tap
    - ksdme/tap
    - olets/tap

  # Command-line tools (Homebrew formulas)
  brews:
    # Essential tools for any system (base layer)
    # Includes core shell utilities and modern CLI replacements
    base:
      - atuin
      - bat
      - bottom
      - btop
      - curl
      - difftastic
      - duf
      - dust
      - eza

    # Infrastructure and Container-oriented tools
    container:
      - awscli
      - ddev
      - helm
      - kubernetes-cli
      - opentofu

    # Full development stack including languages and AI agents
    full:
      - direnv
      - ffmpeg
      - gh
      - go
      - hub
      - hyperfine
      - jira-cli
      - lazygit
      - llm
      - node
      - opencode

      - python3
      - repomix
      - rtk

      - yt-dlp

    # Extra CLI tools for non-transient machines (standard power-user suite)
    standard_cli:
      - broot
      - cheat
      - coder
      - curlie
      - doggo
      - dyff
      - fx
      - git-extras
      - git-quick-stats

      - mkcert

      - pinentry
      - playwright-cli
      - pv
      - specify
      - superfile
      - tailspin
      - the_silver_searcher
      - ut
      - watch
      - xxh
      - xz

    # macOS specific formulas
    macos:
      - mas
      - pinentry-mac

  # macOS Casks (GUI apps and some CLI tools)
  casks:
    # CLI-only tools distributed as casks (preferred on macOS)
    cli:
      - 1password-cli
      - claude-code
      - entire

    # Fonts (Installed if macos=true)
    fonts:
      - font-0xproto-nerd-font
      - font-cascadia-code
      - font-cascadia-code-pl
      - font-cascadia-mono
      - font-cascadia-mono-pl
      - font-caskaydia-cove-nerd-font
      - font-droid-sans-mono-nerd-font
      - font-fira-code-nerd-font
      - font-go-mono-nerd-font
      - font-hack
      - font-hack-nerd-font
      - font-inconsolata
      - font-inconsolata-nerd-font
      - font-jetbrains-mono
      - font-jetbrains-mono-nerd-font
      - font-monaspice-nerd-font

    # Essential GUI (Installed if macos=true)
    macos:
      - 1password
      - choosy
      - comet
      - ghostty
      - google-chrome
      - sublime-text

    # Personal GUI apps (Installed if personal=true)
    personal:
      - claude
      - dayflow
      - disk-inventory-x
      - divvy
      - handbrake-app
      - karabiner-elements
      - logseq
      - loop
      - maccy
      - macshot
      - mounty
      - notion
      - notion-calendar
      - obsidian
      - raycast
      - rectangle
      - shortcat
      - slack
      - sloth
      - stats
      - tailscale-app
      - the-unarchiver
      - vlc
      - wispr-flow
      - zoom

    # Developer-specific GUI tools (Installed if stack=full)
    development:
      - gitup-app
      - imageoptim
      - orbstack
      - visual-studio-code
      - warp
      - zed

    # Large applications and hardware drivers (Installed if workstation=true)
    workstation:
      - antigravity
      - elgato-control-center
      - elgato-stream-deck
      - gnucash
      - logi-options-plus
      - microsoft-office
      - microsoft-teams
      - whatsapp

  # APT packages (fallback or bootstrap for Linux)
  apt:
    - curl
    - git
    - fish
    - zsh

  # Linux-specific configurations (fallbacks for when Casks/Brews don't apply)
  linux:
    npm:
      - "@anthropic-ai/claude-code"
      - "@google/gemini-cli"
    scripts:
      - name: entire
        command: curl -fsSL https://entire.io/install.sh | bash

  # Generic tool initialization
  init:
    rtk:
      - rtk init -g
      - rtk init -g --gemini
      - rtk init --agent antigravity

thoughts.subject: Investigating Package Managers
thoughts.description: I'm reviewing the package management logic. It appears `npm` isn't directly installable via `apt` in the current setup, though `node` is present in `brews.full`. My focus is now on how the script handles package installation across different environments, particularly the preference for `apt` on Linux.

thoughts.subject: Verifying Script Logic
thoughts.description: I'm digging into the `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl` script. It appears the logic for installing global NPM packages on Linux (`stack: "full"`) relies on `npm` being present, but it's not being installed via `apt`. I'm now focused on how this dependency is managed and if an alternative installation method for `npm` itself is needed.

thoughts.subject: Resolving npm Path Issues
thoughts.description: I've identified a critical discrepancy: while the script indicates `npm` is available, `sudo npm` fails, suggesting a path issue between user and root environments. I'm investigating how `mise` might be managing `node` and `npm`, and how to correctly invoke global installations without `sudo`, as `mise`-managed binaries are typically user-specific.

thoughts.subject: Clarifying Package Sources
thoughts.description: I'm realizing that on Linux, the script *only* uses `apt` for installations, not `brew`. This means if `node` isn't available via `apt`, the subsequent `npm` global installs will indeed fail, especially with `sudo`. My focus is now on this apparent gap for Linux environments.

My focus is now on how the script handles package installation across different environments, particularly the preference for `apt` on Linux.

I'm now focused on how this dependency is managed and if an alternative installation method for `npm` itself is needed.

thoughts.subject: Resolving npm Path Issues
thoughts.description: I've identified a critical discrepancy: while the script indicates `npm` is available, `sudo npm` fails, suggesting a path issue between user and root environments. I'm investigating how `mise` might be managing `node` and `npm`, and how to correctly invoke global installations without `sudo`, as `mise`-managed binaries are typically user-specific.

thoughts.subject: Clarifying Package Sources
thoughts.description: I'm realizing that on Linux, the script *only* uses `apt` for installations, not `brew`. This means if `node` isn't available via `apt`, the subsequent `npm` global installs will indeed fail, especially with `sudo`. My focus is now on this apparent gap for Linux environments.

response.output:
#!/bin/sh

# -e: exit on error
# -u: exit on unset variables
set -eu

# Install Homebrew if not present
if [ ! "$(command -v brew)" ]; then
	echo 'Installing Homebrew...'
	/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
	
	# Load Homebrew for the current session so the rest of this script can use it
	if [ -d /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew ]; then
		# Linux
		eval "$(/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
	elif [ -d /opt/homebrew ]; then
		# macOS (Apple Silicon)
		eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
	else
		# macOS (Intel)
		eval "$(/usr/local/bin/brew shellenv)"
	fi
	
	echo '==================='
	echo 'Homebrew installed and loaded.'
	echo '==================='
fi

if ! chezmoi="$(command -v chezmoi)"; then
	echo "Installing chezmoi..." >&2
	brew install chezmoi
	chezmoi="$(command -v chezmoi)"
fi

# POSIX way to get script's dir
script_dir="$(cd -P -- "$(dirname -- "$(command -v -- "$0")")" && pwd -P)"

set -- init --apply --source="${script_dir}"

echo "Running 'chezmoi $*'" >&2
exec "$chezmoi" "$@"

thoughts.description: I'm currently examining how the installation scripts handle different operating systems. My focus is on the unexpected Homebrew installation on Linux via `install.sh` and how this conflicts with the conditional logic in `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl`. I need to understand if `brew` is intended to be present on Linux in this setup.

thoughts.subject: Investigating Dependency Mismatches
thoughts.description: I'm reviewing the logic around package installation, specifically how `brew` is being invoked on Linux despite the template explicitly favoring `apt` and `npm` for that OS. I'm also looking into the immediate `npm` error on Ubuntu where the command exists but `sudo` fails.

thoughts.subject: Investigating sudo issue
thoughts.description: I'm currently pinpointing why `sudo npm` might be failing on Linux, even if `npm` itself is accessible. My focus is on the interaction between `sudo` and `npm` within the `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl` script.

thoughts.subject: Exploring package manager conflicts
thoughts.description: I'm investigating a potential issue where `npm` might not be properly installed or recognized under `sudo` on Linux systems, despite appearing accessible otherwise. My current hypothesis is that the `node` package itself might be missing from the `apt-get` install list, leading to this discrepancy.

thoughts.subject: Diagnosing path discrepancies
thoughts.description: I'm delving into why `npm` isn't found by `sudo` on Linux. My theory is that version managers like `mise` or `nvm` install `npm` in user-specific paths that `sudo` can't access. I'm considering how to handle this, perhaps by checking for Homebrew on Linux as an alternative.

thoughts.subject: Refining NPM Install Logic
thoughts.description: I'm refining the `npm` installation logic. It seems `sudo` is indeed required for global installs on fresh Ubuntu systems if `npm` is installed via `apt`. However, many users advise against `sudo npm`. I'm exploring modifying the script to check if `npm` is globally writable first, and only then consider using `sudo`, or perhaps omitting it entirely if it's not a system-managed `npm`.

thoughts.subject: Unifying Node Installation
thoughts.description: I'm realizing `node` isn't consistently installed across platforms. On Linux, it's missing from `apt` but might be installed via `brew` if `install.sh` sets it up. I need to unify this or ensure `node` is correctly handled for a "full" stack experience.

thoughts.subject: Adjusting Package Installation
thoughts.description: I'm realizing the script implicitly assumes `brew` is the primary package manager for Linux too, and I should update `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl` accordingly. This might resolve the `npm` issue as `brew` handles its pathing differently than system packages.

thoughts.subject: Clarifying Brew Node Behavior
thoughts.description: I'm realizing `brew` node installs are typically user-writable, negating the need for `sudo npm install -g`. My focus is now on ensuring the `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl` correctly leverages `brew` for Linux `node` and `npm` without unnecessary `sudo`.

thoughts.subject: Simplifying sudo Usage
thoughts.description: I'm shifting focus to unifying `node` and `npm` installation across platforms, ideally avoiding `sudo` where possible. My current approach is to leverage `brew` for Linux installs, as it handles pathing more gracefully and avoids the `sudo npm` issue I encountered with system packages.

thoughts.subject: Integrating brew on Linux
thoughts.description: I'm confirming that `install.sh` reliably ensures `brew` is available on Linux. This means I can simplify the script, always utilizing `brew` for CLI tool installation regardless of the OS, as the script's logic can now assume its presence after the initial setup.

thoughts.subject: Simplifying npm installs

thoughts.subject: Refining privilege handling
thoughts.description: I'm re-evaluating how I handle `npm` installations on Linux. If `brew` installs Node, `npm` should be accessible without `sudo` in most cases. I'm adjusting the script to detect writability before resorting to `sudo`, preventing potential "command not found" errors by avoiding unnecessary privilege escalation when it's not required.

thoughts.subject: Verifying path for npm
thoughts.description: I'm confirming that the `npm` command is accessible without `sudo` after Homebrew installs Node. The error suggests a PATH issue within the subshell, which should be resolved as `chezmoi` should inherit the environment after `eval $(brew shellenv)`.

thoughts.subject: Confirming Linux package logic

toolCalls.name: update_topic

args.strategic_intent: Refactoring the installation script to support Homebrew on Linux and fix NPM permissions.
args.title: Refactoring installation script
args.summary: I have identified that the `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl` script incorrectly limits Homebrew package installation to macOS, despite the `install.sh` script setting up Homebrew on Linux. Additionally, the script uses `sudo npm install -g` which fails when `npm` is installed via a user-level manager like `mise` or `nvm` (as `sudo` does not inherit the user's PATH). I will refactor the script to use Homebrew on both platforms and implement a smarter check for `npm` to avoid unnecessary or failing `sudo` calls.

Additionally, the script uses `sudo npm install -g` which fails when `npm` is installed via a user-level manager like `mise` or `nvm` (as `sudo` does not inherit the user's PATH). I will refactor the script to use Homebrew on both platforms and implement a smarter check for `npm` to avoid unnecessary or failing `sudo` calls.

Strategic Intent: Refactoring the installation script to support Homebrew on Linux and fix NPM permissions.

toolCalls.resultDisplay:
## 📂 Topic: **Refactoring installation script**

**Summary:**
I have identified that the `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl` script incorrectly limits Homebrew package installation to macOS, despite the `install.sh` script setting up Homebrew on Linux. Additionally, the script uses `sudo npm install -g` which fails when `npm` is installed via a user-level manager like `mise` or `nvm` (as `sudo` does not inherit the user's PATH). I will refactor the script to use Homebrew on both platforms and implement a smarter check for `npm` to avoid unnecessary or failing `sudo` calls.

> [!STRATEGY]
> **Intent:** Refactoring the installation script to support Homebrew on Linux and fix NPM permissions.

thoughts.subject: Revising Script Logic
thoughts.description: I've just finished refactoring the script and am now meticulously checking for any lingering issues. My attention is particularly focused on `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl`, where I've identified potential areas for improvement based on prior observations regarding the `.chezmoidata/packages.yaml` hash.

thoughts.subject: Validating Install Paths
thoughts.description: I'm currently analyzing potential conflicts with existing Node.js installations on Linux when using Brew. My focus is on ensuring the script gracefully handles situations where Node might already be present, preventing unexpected `sudo npm` failures due to pathing issues.

thoughts.description: I've just finished refactoring the script and am now meticulously checking for any lingering issues. My attention is particularly focused on `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl`, where I've identified potential areas for improvement based on prior observations regarding the `.chezmoidata/packages.yaml` hash.

#!/usr/bin/env bash

# 1. Native installation (Linux only)
# We must keep native installations because some tools (like Warp) expect 
# shells to be at standard system paths (/usr/bin/zsh) for their 
# initialization scripts to work correctly.
{{- if (eq .chezmoi.os "linux") }}
{{- if or (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "ubuntu") (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "debian") }}
export DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive
# Add fish PPA/Repo if missing
if ! command -v fish &> /dev/null; then
    {{- if (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "ubuntu") }}
    sudo apt-add-repository -y ppa:fish-shell/release-4
    {{- else if (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "debian") }}
    echo "deb http://download.opensuse.org/repositories/shells:/fish:/release:/4/Debian_{{ .chezmoi.osRelease.versionID }}/ /" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/shells:fish:release:4.list
    curl -fsSL https://download.opensuse.org/repositories/shells:fish:release:4/Debian_{{ .chezmoi.osRelease.versionID }}/Release.key | gpg --dearmor | sudo tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/shells_fish_release_4.gpg > /dev/null
    {{- end }}
fi
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get -yq install fish zsh
{{- else if (eq .chezmoi.osRelease.id "fedora") }}
sudo dnf -yq install fish zsh
{{- end }}
{{- end }}

# 2. Homebrew installation (MacOS & Linux)
# Install Homebrew if missing
type brew &> /dev/null || /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

# Ensure Homebrew is in PATH for the rest of this script
if [[ -d /opt/homebrew/bin ]]; then
    eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
elif [[ -d /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin ]]; then
    eval "$(/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin/brew shellenv)"
fi

# Install shells via Homebrew - this provides the latest versions
# coexisting with the native ones.
brew install fish zsh

thoughts.subject: Resolving `npm` issues
thoughts.description: I've successfully addressed the core `npm` problem. It appears the error stemmed from inconsistent use of `sudo npm`, likely due to pathing issues or unnecessary elevation. My refined approach now intelligently determines when `sudo` is truly required for global installations.

thoughts.subject: Resolving `npm` issues
thoughts.description: I've successfully addressed the core `npm` problem. It appears the error stemmed from inconsistent use of `sudo npm`, likely due to pathing issues or unnecessary elevation. My refined approach now intelligently determines when `sudo` is truly required for global installations.

content:
I have fixed the issue where the `chezmoi` installation script was failing on Ubuntu when attempting to install global NPM packages.

### Analysis of the Problem
The error `sudo: 'npm': command not found` occurred because the script was forcing the use of `sudo npm install -g`, but `npm` was likely installed via a user-level version manager (like `mise`, `nvm`, or `asdf`) which is not available in `sudo`'s restricted `PATH`. Additionally, I discovered that the main package installation script was incorrectly skipping all Homebrew packages on Linux, even though the project is set up to use Homebrew on both macOS and Linux.

### Changes Made
1.  **Unified Homebrew Support:** Refactored `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl` to enable Homebrew package installation on both macOS and Linux. This ensures that tools like `node`, `go`, and `mise` are correctly installed via Homebrew on your Ubuntu VM when the "full" stack is selected.
2.  **Robust Homebrew PATH Initialization:** Added logic to automatically detect and load Homebrew's environment variables (`brew shellenv`) at the start of the script, ensuring `brew` is available even if it was just installed.
3.  **Smart NPM Permissions:** Implemented a check to determine if `sudo` is actually needed for global NPM installations. If the NPM prefix is writable by the current user (which is the case for Homebrew, `mise`, or `nvm` installations), it will now skip `sudo`, avoiding the "command not found" error.
4.  **Linux/macOS Logical Separation:** Kept GUI applications (Casks) and macOS-specific formulas restricted to Darwin while allowing shared CLI tools (Brews) to be installed on both platforms.

You should now be able to run `chezmoi init` or `chezmoi apply` again, and it should proceed without the `npm` error.

thoughts.subject: Refining Script Execution
thoughts.description: The script appears robust. I've confirmed Homebrew on Linux doesn't require `sudo`. I'm currently considering potential prompts from `brew install` and how to handle them gracefully for a seamless user experience.

content:
I have fixed the issue where the `chezmoi` installation script was failing on Ubuntu when attempting to install global NPM packages.

### Analysis of the Problem
The error `sudo: 'npm': command not found` occurred because the script was forcing the use of `sudo npm install -g`, but `npm` was likely installed via a user-level version manager (like `mise`, `nvm`, or `asdf`) which is not available in `sudo`'s restricted `PATH`. Additionally, I discovered that the main package installation script was incorrectly skipping all Homebrew packages on Linux, even though the project is set up to use Homebrew on both macOS and Linux.

### Changes Made
1.  **Unified Homebrew Support:** Refactored `run_onchange_install_packages.sh.tmpl` to enable Homebrew package installation on both macOS and Linux. This ensures that tools like `node`, `go`, and `mise` are correctly installed via Homebrew on your Ubuntu VM when the "full" stack is selected.
2.  **Robust Homebrew PATH Initialization:** Added logic to automatically detect and load Homebrew's environment variables (`brew shellenv`) at the start of the script, ensuring `brew` is available even if it was just installed.
3.  **Smart NPM Permissions:** Implemented a check to determine if `sudo` is actually needed for global NPM installations. If the NPM prefix is writable by the current user (which is the case for Homebrew, `mise`, or `nvm` installations), it will now skip `sudo`, avoiding the "command not found" error.
4.  **Linux/macOS Logical Separation:** Kept GUI applications (Casks) and macOS-specific formulas restricted to Darwin while allowing shared CLI tools (Brews) to be installed on both platforms.

You should now be able to run `chezmoi init` or `chezmoi apply` again, and it should proceed without the `npm` error.

content:
I have fixed the issue where the `chezmoi` installation script was failing on Ubuntu.

### Summary of Fixes
- **NPM Permission Handling:** Modified the script to check if the NPM global directory is writable by the current user. It now only uses `sudo` if necessary, which prevents the `sudo: 'npm': command not found` error when using user-level Node.js managers like `mise` or `nvm`.
- **Homebrew on Linux:** Enabled Homebrew package installation for Linux. Previously, the script was skipping all `brew` formulas on Linux, which meant many tools (including `node`) were not being installed as expected.
- **Environment Robustness:** Added automatic Homebrew environment detection to ensures the `brew` command is always in the `PATH` during the installation process.

You can now re-run your `chezmoi` initialization:
```bash
chezmoi apply
```

toolCalls.name: activate_skill

response.output:
<activated_skill name="conventional-commits">
  <instructions>
    # Conventional Commits

Write every commit message according to the [Conventional Commits v1.0.0](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/) specification.

## Undercover mode

**CRITICAL:** Do NOT add `Co-Authored-By`, `Signed-off-by`, or any other trailer that attributes or identifies an AI agent as a contributor. The commit must look like it was written entirely by the human developer. This applies to all footers — never reveal agent involvement.

## Commit message format

```
<type>[optional scope]: <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

## Rules

### Type (required)

The first element is the **type** — a noun that categorizes the change:

| Type       | When to use                                          | SemVer effect |
|------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------|
| `feat`     | A new feature for the user or consumer               | MINOR         |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                            | PATCH         |
| `docs`     | Documentation only                                   | -             |
| `style`    | Formatting, whitespace, semicolons — no logic change | -             |
| `refactor` | Code change that neither fixes a bug nor adds a feature | -          |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                              | -             |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                           | -             |
| `build`    | Changes to the build system or dependencies          | -             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration and scripts                         | -             |
| `chore`    | Other changes that don't modify src or test files    | -             |

Only `feat` and `fix` are mandated by the spec. The rest are widely adopted conventions. Pick the type that most accurately describes the change — do not default to `chore` when a more specific type applies.

### Scope (optional)

A noun in parentheses after the type describing the section of the codebase affected:

```
feat(auth): add OAuth2 login flow
fix(parser): handle empty input without crashing
```

Use scope when the repo is large enough that it helps orient the reader. Keep scopes consistent within a project — check `git log` for existing scopes before inventing new ones.

### Description (required)

- Immediately follows the colon and space after the type/scope prefix
- Use the imperative mood ("add", "fix", "remove" — not "added", "fixes", "removed")
- Do not capitalize the first letter
- Do not end with a period
- Keep it under 72 characters
- Summarize *what* the change does, not *how*

### Body (optional)

- Separated from the description by one blank line
- Free-form — can contain multiple paragraphs
- Use to explain *why* the change was made when the description alone is insufficient
- Wrap lines at 72 characters

### Footers (optional)

- Separated from the body (or description if no body) by one blank line
- Format: `token: value` or `token #value`
- Tokens use `-` instead of spaces (e.g., `Reviewed-by`, `Refs`)
- Exception: `BREAKING CHANGE` (must be uppercase, space allowed)

### Breaking changes

Indicate breaking changes in one of two ways (or both):

1. **`!` after type/scope:** `feat(api)!: remove deprecated endpoints`
2. **Footer:** `BREAKING CHANGE: the /v1 endpoints have been removed`

A `BREAKING CHANGE` footer MUST be uppercase. When present, it triggers a MAJOR version bump regardless of the type.

## Choosing the right type

Before writing the message, examine the staged changes:

1. **Does it add new user-facing behavior?** -> `feat`
2. **Does it fix incorrect behavior?** -> `fix`
3. **Does it only change documentation?** -> `docs`
4. **Does it only change tests?** -> `test`
5. **Does it improve performance without changing behavior?** -> `perf`
6. **Does it restructure code without changing behavior?** -> `refactor`
7. **Does it only change formatting/style?** -> `style`
8. **Does it change build tooling or dependencies?** -> `build`
9. **Does it change CI pipelines?** -> `ci`
10. **None of the above?** -> `chore`

If a commit spans multiple types, prefer the most significant one (usually `feat` or `fix`). If the changes are truly unrelated, consider splitting into separate commits.

## Workflow

When asked to commit, or when you determine a commit is needed:

1. Run `git diff --cached` (or `git diff` if nothing is staged) to understand what changed.
2. Run `git log --oneline -10` to see recent commit style and any existing scope conventions.
3. Classify the change using the type selection guide above.
4. Write the commit message following the format rules.
5. Use `write_file` to save the message to a temporary file (e.g., `.git/COMMIT_MESSAGE`) and then use `git commit -F` to commit. This is the most robust way to handle multi-line messages and avoid safety blocks when the message content contains shell-sensitive characters like `$(` or backticks:

```bash
# Step 1: write_file(file_path=".git/COMMIT_MESSAGE", content="...")
# Step 2:
git commit -F .git/COMMIT_MESSAGE && rm .git/COMMIT_MESSAGE
```

## Examples

Simple feature:
```
feat: add email notifications for failed jobs
```

Bug fix with scope:
```
fix(css): correct button alignment on mobile viewports
```

Breaking change with `!` and footer:
```
feat(api)!: require authentication for all endpoints

All API endpoints now require a valid Bearer token.
Previously, read-only endpoints were publicly accessible.

BREAKING CHANGE: unauthenticated requests to any endpoint now return 401
```

Refactor with body:
```
refactor: extract validation logic into shared module

The same validation rules were duplicated across three controllers.
Moving them to a shared module reduces drift and makes the rules
easier to test in isolation.
```

Docs only:
```
docs: add contributing guidelines for new developers
```

Build change:
```
build(deps): upgrade webpack from 4 to 5
```
  </instructions>

  <available_resources>
    Showing up to 200 items (files + folders).

/Users/hw/.agents/skills/conventional-commits/
├───SKILL.md
└───evals/
    └───evals.json
  </available_resources>
</activated_skill>

args.content:
fix(install): support Homebrew on Linux and improve NPM permission handling

- Enable Homebrew package installation on Linux systems.
- Add robust Homebrew PATH detection (brew shellenv) for Linux and macOS.
- Implement smart NPM permission check to skip sudo when the prefix is writable.
- Refactor package installation logic to be more cross-platform.

This fixes the 'sudo: npm: command not found' error on Ubuntu systems
where npm is installed via user-level version managers.

I have successfully investigated and fixed the installation error on Ubuntu. The root cause was a combination of missing Homebrew support for Linux and a forced `sudo npm` command that failed in user-managed Node.js environments. I refactored the installation script, verified the changes, and committed them with a descriptive commit message.

> [!STRATEGY]
> **Intent:** Finalizing the task.

content:
The changes have been committed to the repository.

### Changes Summary:
- **`fix(install): support Homebrew on Linux and improve NPM permission handling`**
  - **Enabled Homebrew on Linux:** Corrected the script to allow Homebrew package installation on Linux systems, ensuring tools like `node` are available.
  - **Robust Path Detection:** Added logic to automatically source `brew shellenv` for Linux and both Apple Silicon/Intel Macs.
  - **Smart NPM Sudo:** Replaced forced `sudo npm` with a check that only uses `sudo` if the installation directory is not writable by the current user. This fixes the error on systems using `mise`, `nvm`, or Homebrew-managed Node.js.

The fix is now ready for use on your Ubuntu VM.
